metadata.title: Rewind, Not Fix-Forward: The Andon Cord

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa, Liam — in for Bear. This is Liam, in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Liam, in for Bear?
props.segment: Rewind, Not Fix-Forward: The Andon Cord

beats.narration_text: The test passed. The code was wrong. `deleteApplication` used splice instead of filter — it mutated the input array. The handoff condition was three tests pass, and the tests were blind to mutation. The tests passed with splice. They also pass with filter. Seth pasted a correction: 'the delete function should be immutable, please fix it.' The next output fixed delete. It broke toggle. The context already contained the misunderstanding. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, correction prompt after splice bug

props.command: the delete function isn't immutable, please fix it

beats.narration_text: Forward correction appends to a context that already contains the misunderstanding. The corrective prompt does not replace the failed prompt — it joins it. Claude's next output is conditioned on both. Fix-forward usually resolves the named symptom and introduces a new one elsewhere, because the underlying spec gap is still there, just further down the conversation.
beats.new_visual_element: Forward correction diagram — failed prompt + correction = conditioned on both

beats.narration_text: Rewind: Esc-Esc in Claude Code, or the `/rewind` command. Both restore conversation and code to the state before the last prompt. You are not rolling back the symptom — you are rolling back the cause. The prompt that produced the misunderstanding disappears. The files Claude wrote disappear. You are back to before the wrong move. Then you respecify.
beats.new_visual_element: Esc-Esc mechanics — state restored to pre-prompt / files gone / respecify

beats.narration_text: The respecify step is where the value is. The mutation bug existed because the handoff condition did not test for immutability. The rewind creates space to add one sentence to the prompt: 'use only immutable updates — return new arrays; do not mutate the input.' The context is shorter now than it was before the failed attempt. Context past a point degrades quality — rewind shortens it. Forward correction lengthens it.
beats.new_visual_element: Respecify — one added sentence closes the spec gap; shorter context = better output

beats.narration_text: Two rewinds on the same row for the same failure is a signal: the problem is not in Claude's output, it is in the spec. Stop. Open the handoff condition and the SDD side by side. Ask: is this condition testing what I want, or what I was able to write down? Usually one answer is yes, and the fix is in the document, not in the next prompt. Rewind is the keystroke equivalent of Toyota's andon cord. Stop the line. Fix the cause.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — two-rewind signal: fix the spec, not the prompt

beats.narration_text: Next time an output passes the test and still feels wrong — rewind and respecify. Paste this first.

props.command: Before I rewind: help me identify what the spec is missing. The output I rejected: [describe it]. What it passed: [handoff condition]. What felt wrong: [describe]. Give me: (1) the constraint that was absent from the original prompt, (2) a revised handoff condition that would catch this class of error, (3) the one sentence to add to the respecified prompt.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Rewind, Not Fix-Forward: The Andon Cord] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
